metadata.title: Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object in Journalism Studies

abstract: Mainstream media" and "mainstream journalism" have become significant concepts in political, popular, and academic discourse in recent years. They are key in how media, communication, and journalism scholars have been, and continue to be, concerned with relational dimensions in their work. However, the meaning of the word "mainstream" is often taken for granted. How and by whom this concept is used among media, communication, and journalism scholars is therefore of great importance. This article analyses uses of "mainstream" in academic discourse to unpack the various meanings ascribed to it. Through computational Topic Modelling Analysis and qualitative context analysis we analyse the relations, positions, normative underpinnings, and appropriate uses of "mainstream" in 779 journal articles published between 2000 and 2022. The analysis is framed by Wittgenstein's notion of "language-game" as a way of analysing the meaning-making process constituted by uses of "mainstream" in these articles. We argue that "mainstream" resembles a "boundary object" but conclude that the rules of the "mainstream language-game" are confusing and need revisions, and that the concept only functions as an illusory boundary object, which is arguably problematic.

paragraphs: One of the core characteristics of digital journalism studies as a scholarly field is its emphasis on the crumbling borders between previously distinctive organisations, professions, and practices within the domain of public discourse (Carlson 2015(Carlson , 2017;;Carlson et al. 2018;Eldridge 2018;Steensen and Westlund 2021;Waisbord 2013). This emphasis has pushed concepts like mainstream, alternative, fringe, centre, periphery, interlopers, etc. to the forefront of the field. Such concepts not only draw attention to the relationships between various actors, practices, and activities within public discourse and how those relationships change but are alsowe argueoften enmeshed with certain normative interpretations which define and categorise those relationships according to assumptions of what journalism is or ought to be, how and by whom it is practised.

This article discusses one of the most important and perhaps also most ambiguous of these relational and normative concepts, namely "mainstream." This metaphor, especially the acronym MSM, has become politically weaponized by far-right populist politics and publics in recent years, predominantly in the USA, but also elsewhere. It is a contested concept, which meaning is "open for debate" (Cushion, McDowell-Naylor, and Thomas 2021, 635), and which floats in and between the social worlds of politics, publics, journalism, and academia. Whether and to what extent scholars in media, communication, and journalism studies take this into account, address it, or rather use the concept of mainstream media without definition or explicit contextualisation is the starting point for this article. A main concern is that discussions and analysis in the academic literature of relations in the media ecology and between media and politics can become as ambiguous as the concept of "mainstream" itself. We believe it is especially important to raise this concern within the context of digital journalism studies, because this is a field in which contestations and deconstructions of core concepts and understandings of what "media," "journalism" etc. actually mean constitute a defining characteristic (Steensen and Westlund 2021;Ahva and Steensen 2017). In fact, we argue that the various meanings of "mainstream" and the contexts within scholarship in which the concept is used is so important that it seems to constitute a "boundary object" (Star 1988;Star and Griesemer 1989)a shared "object" which embeds key contestations, demarcates borders between various actors and practices, and creates a space for seemingly shared meaning.

The aim of this article is to analyse the degrees to which "mainstream" functions as such a boundary object in academic discourse on media and journalism. We analyse uses of the concept in journal articles published in media, communication, and journalism journals from 2000 and onwards. This analysis is based on Wittgenstein's ([1953] 2009) philosophy of language, his idea that the meaning of words is connected to use -"meaning as use"and that understanding the meaning of this use implies an analysis of communicative actions as language-games. In other words: Our purpose is to unveil the characteristics of "mainstream" as a potential boundary object in academic discourse through an analysis of the mainstream language-game as it is played in journal articles.

The article starts with unpacking what we mean by the mainstream language-game and how this serves as a way to operationalise an analysis of the academic discourse in which "mainstream" is a key concept. We then discuss "mainstream" as a potential boundary object, before we present our two-step methodology: a quantitative, automated topic modelling analysis of journal article abstracts in which the phrases "mainstream media" or "mainstream journalis Ã " appear, and a qualitative context analysis of uses of "mainstream" in articles published in the main journalism journals from 2011 to 2022. After discussing our findings, we conclude that the rules of the mainstream language-game are confusing and need revisions, and that the concept only functions as an illusory boundary object, which we find problematic, given its increased significance.

"Mainstream" as a Language Game Used as a metaphor, "mainstream" has a myriad of potential meanings. It originates from a political context in the United States, where New York governor Nelson Rockefeller during the 1960s spoke about the "mainstream of political thought and action" (Safire 2008, 408). Rockefeller used the concept as a positive standard, something of high quality that other things could be measured and tested against. The phrase "mainstream media" was, according to Safire (2008, 209), coined by former US presidential candidate Ralph Nader in 1985. Nader first used it as a rather descriptive phrase, but later connected it with a critical perspective on media favouring the powerful elite and suppressing alternative, critical voices, and minority perspectives (Nader 2015). This understanding is similar to the one Chomsky discussed when he in 1997 asked "what makes mainstream media mainstream?." Chomsky's answer was enmeshed with critical perspectives on elitism, power preservation, and market logic, and that mainstream media will only "reflect the interests of the buyers and sellers, the institutions, and the power systems that are around them" (Chomsky 1997).

In recent years, the metaphor has been connected with aggressive far-right populist rhetoric associated with former US president Donald Trump, who notoriously talked about "mainstream media" with unmasked antipathy, often in connection with phrases like "fake news" and "lamestream media." This aggressive, political rhetoric and the relations set up by it between what is mainstream and what is not, has to varying degrees been exported to other cultural contexts beyond the USA. But "mainstream media" no doubt refers to different media in different contexts, and the relations to political rhetoric will vary across and within different media systems.

"Mainstream" can be used as a descriptive concept, simply referring to the biggest and most influential news media in a media system, and as a normative concept, reflecting power dynamics and media criticism coming from all sides of the political spectrum. And both these uses will carry different meanings in different cultural and political contexts. To unpack these meanings, one must therefore look at how and in what contexts the concept is used. In language studies, analysing the meaning of words through their use is the starting point of speech act theory, in which a key premise is that one cannot derive the meaning of words inductively from some theory of meaning-making alone. Words and language are not "bloodless abstract objects that can be studied like specimens under a microscope" (Lycan 2018, 76). Words get their meaning in connection with behaviour, with the social practice of communication. Our investigation of uses of "mainstream" is guided by a particular view on how meaning-making happens in language. This view is based on Wittgenstein's philosophy of language and his notion of "language-games" (Wittgenstein [1953] 2009).

Wittgenstein's idea of meaning-making as a language-game implies that what we do when we use language in social acts of communication, is to make moves, and that these moves are governed by rules, just like the rules of other games. Wittgenstein's use of "game" should be interpreted metaphorically. Black (1979, 350) argues that language-game "should be regarded as an Idiosyncratic term of art derived from ordinary uses of, say, 'game with words', by a deliberate extension of application." One important difference between language-games and other games, like football or chess, is that in language-games the rules are very complex and rarely articulated. We learn the rules of language-games as we learn to speak. We learn that certain acts of communication, certain moves, are appropriate responses to certain other communicative moves. The phrase "You're welcome" is, for instance, an appropriate response to the phrase "Thank you", and it gets its meaning as a response to the "Thank you." Such rules, which regulate what is considered appropriate responses to certain acts of communication are essential for the communication to make sense. Without relations to other communicative moves, a single speech-act rarely makes much sense. Meaning, thereby, is the function words and phrases perform in the context of social practice. This is also the case with a word like "mainstream." This word predominantly carries meaning through its relations with other speech acts in a given social context. Knowledge of both the context of use and the relations to other speech acts is therefore essential to understand what "mainstream" means. Viewing uses of "mainstream" as communicative moves in language-games implies an emphasis on the purpose of the move within the given context, and an evaluation of it (Deitz and Arrington 1984, 5). Like moves in a game, communicative actions are goal oriented. We must assume that scholars in media, communication, and journalism studies (and any other actor for that matter) use the term "mainstream" with intent. Its use serves a specific purpose, and revealing this purpose is one of the aims of analysing the mainstream language-game. Moreover, like moves in any other game, uses of mainstream can be evaluated as appropriate or in-appropriate. Appropriate moves follow the rules of the game, while in-appropriate moves violate the rules.

In our analysis of the mainstream language-game, as it is played in published journal articles, we take for granted that the uses of mainstream are already evaluated as appropriate through peer-review processes and editorial evaluation and that these uses thereby adhere to the rules of the mainstream language-game. Published journal articles not only follow the rules of academic publishing, they also follow the rules of the many language-games embedded in them, related to the topics they treat and the key words they apply. As such, published journal articles follow the rules of the field of academic publishing also in a Bourdieuian sense, implying that belonging to such a field means adhering to its rules and sharing "implicit schemes of classification" (Bourdieu 2005, 36). Uses of "mainstream" in the context of academic publishing could resemble such an implicit classification, which would mean that the relational aspects of its use are more implicit than explicit. Uses of "mainstream" can in such cases refer to an in-field common-sensicle meaning of the word which media, communication, and journalism scholars assume is recognized by their colleagues as part of the field's doxa. This does not mean that the concept is used in a non-relational way, it only means that relational dimensions are implicit. In our analysis, we will therefore look for such implicit communicative moves.

Given this premise that the uses of "mainstream" in published journal articles are already deemed appropriate, an analysis of how the mainstream language-game is played in this context will reveal the rules that govern this game. Such an analysis will potentially also reveal if the rules have changed, and if there are conflicting rules, which would suggest that there is not one mainstream language-game, but several. It follows from the aforementioned that analysing uses of mainstream as a language-game will require taking the following aspectswhich are also proposed by Deitz and Arrington's (1984, 5) operationalisation of language-game analysisinto account:

The interrelated actions of uses of "mainstream" and other speech acts, the context of use, the purpose of the use, and the appropriate mode of evaluation. We address these aspects through an analysis of the following research question: RQ1: What are the topical contexts and relations in which "mainstream" follows an "appropriate" language-game use in media, communication, and journalism studies research?

"Mainstream" as a Boundary Object Analysing uses of mainstream as a language-game only makes sense if this game is important in a broader context. There are several reasons why we believe the mainstream language-game is important. First, it points to some key characteristics of the news media: who and what is considered at the centre and who and what is not. Second, the word "mainstream" is often associated with words like "elite," "power," and "hegemony" (Chomsky 1997), or with words like "traditional" and "established" (Cushion, McDowell-Naylor, and Thomas 2021) and its use can therefore potentially reveal something about perceptions of such dimensions of the news media. Third, "mainstream" is also connected to political discourse, not only because the word's origin as a metaphor lies with such a discourse, but also because it has been further politicised in recent years by the alternative right movement (Steppat, Castro, and Esser 2021). Fourth, "mainstream" is a common word also in popular discourse and with actors in the media industry, which means it is used in multiple language-games also outside of academia and politics. "Mainstream" is therefore a word that seems to create a link between academic, professional, political, and popular discourse and the various actors involved in all those discourses. As such, it seems to constitute a shared space for communicative actionseven though the word might mean different things to the different actors and in differing contexts. This is exactly the characteristic of what Star labelled a "boundary object" (Star 1988;Star and Griesemer 1989). Boundary objects are "objects which are both plastic enough to adapt to local needs and the constraints of the several parties employing them, yet robust enough to maintain a common identity across sites." They have "different meanings in different social worlds but their structure is common enough to more than one world to make them recognizable, a means of translation" (Star and Griesemer 1989, 393). "Mainstream" might serve as such a flexible translator between social worlds. It seems to have a common-sensical meaning, which makes it identifiable across contexts, and it is filled with additional layers of meaning when used in local contexts. This flexibility ideally allows discourses in academia, politics, the media industry, and among the general public around for instance news media and power to overlap.

Boundary object was originally used as an analytical concept in studies of cooperative work in the absence of consensus, specifically how various professional groups managed to cooperate despite their different viewpoints (Star and Griesemer 1989).

Since its original use more than 30 years ago, "boundary object" has been applied to the analysis of many kinds of situations, practices, and socio-cultural phenomena in different academic disciplines and fields, including analysis of the meaning of words and phrases in academic scholarship. Examples include analysis of "Energy justice" as a boundary object in energy research (Iwi nska, Lis, and Ma˛czka 2021), "resilience" across academic disciplines (Baggio, Brown, and Hellebrandt 2015), "stewardship" in sustainability research (Pec ¸anha Enqvist et al. 2018), and "science communication" as a boundary object connecting science and nonscience (Horst 2022). Star (2010, 612-613) argues that the concept of boundary objects is most useful at the organisational level, where the work arrangements that form around them can be studied. This points to the origin of the concept within ethnographic fieldwork and that boundary objects are most relevant as something people act in relation to, in a defined social context. As such, treating "mainstream" as a potential boundary object connecting the social worlds of news production, academic work, politics, and popular discourse might be perceived as stretching the concept too far, scaled up to such a degree that its analytical power collapses, like a balloon filled with too much air. However, our analysis of mainstream as a possible boundary object is scaled down to the communicative actions within one, limited social world, the one of academic journal publishing in a specific field. The other social worlds to which "mainstream" plays a role are visible as discursive and empirical traces within this limited context of academic publishing. For example, media and journalism scholars might in their publications investigate how actors within or outside of mainstream news media perform or reflect, they might investigate political discourse within or outside of mainstream news media, they might investigate uses and perceptions of mainstream media among publics, and so on and so forth. Moreover, combining boundary object analysis with language-game analysis makes it possible to scale up the analytical ambition and study the "ambiguity of words'' across social worlds (Star 2010, 612).

Our analysis is not a typical boundary object study. We do not do ethnographic fieldwork and as such do not study the work arrangements of the various actors involved in academic publishing and how "mainstream" might work as a boundary object to them. We analyse how "mainstream" as a concept is negotiated and given meaning in academic publications. Meaning can take many forms in this context, ranging from the common-sensical and rather vague meaning that serves as a potential translator between social worlds, to the more specified and nuanced meanings that occur in the local contexts of empirical data. This will partly be addressed by RQ1, but will be further explored through a qualitative analysis of a subset of journal articles within journalism studies journals only suited to address a second research question:
RQ 2: How is the word "mainstream" defined and given nuanced meaning in journalism studies journals?

Viewed together, the two RQs provide findings that address this article's main aim, which is to analyse whether "mainstream" functions as a boundary object in academic discourse on media and journalism.

One of the core characteristics of digital journalism studies as a scholarly field is its emphasis on the crumbling borders between previously distinctive organisations, professions, and practices within the domain of public discourse (Carlson 2015(Carlson , 2017;;Carlson et al. 2018;Eldridge 2018;Steensen and Westlund 2021;Waisbord 2013). Such concepts not only draw attention to the relationships between various actors, practices, and activities within public discourse and how those relationships change but are alsowe argueoften enmeshed with certain normative interpretations which define and categorise those relationships according to assumptions of what journalism is or ought to be, how and by whom it is practised.

This article discusses one of the most important and perhaps also most ambiguous of these relational and normative concepts, namely "mainstream." This metaphor, especially the acronym MSM, has become politically weaponized by far-right populist politics and publics in recent years, predominantly in the USA, but also elsewhere. It is a contested concept, which meaning is "open for debate" (Cushion, McDowell-Naylor, and Thomas 2021, 635), and which floats in and between the social worlds of politics, publics, journalism, and academia. Whether and to what extent scholars in media, communication, and journalism studies take this into account, address it, or rather use the concept of mainstream media without definition or explicit contextualisation is the starting point for this article. A main concern is that discussions and analysis in the academic literature of relations in the media ecology and between media and politics can become as ambiguous as the concept of "mainstream" itself. We believe it is especially important to raise this concern within the context of digital journalism studies, because this is a field in which contestations and deconstructions of core concepts and understandings of what "media," "journalism" etc. actually mean constitute a defining characteristic (Steensen and Westlund 2021;Ahva and Steensen 2017). In fact, we argue that the various meanings of "mainstream" and the contexts within scholarship in which the concept is used is so important that it seems to constitute a "boundary object" (Star 1988;Star and Griesemer 1989)a shared "object" which embeds key contestations, demarcates borders between various actors and practices, and creates a space for seemingly shared meaning.

The aim of this article is to analyse the degrees to which "mainstream" functions as such a boundary object in academic discourse on media and journalism. We analyse uses of the concept in journal articles published in media, communication, and journalism journals from 2000 and onwards. This analysis is based on Wittgenstein's ([1953] 2009) philosophy of language, his idea that the meaning of words is connected to use -"meaning as use"and that understanding the meaning of this use implies an analysis of communicative actions as language-games. In other words: Our purpose is to unveil the characteristics of "mainstream" as a potential boundary object in academic discourse through an analysis of the mainstream language-game as it is played in journal articles.

The article starts with unpacking what we mean by the mainstream language-game and how this serves as a way to operationalise an analysis of the academic discourse in which "mainstream" is a key concept. We then discuss "mainstream" as a potential boundary object, before we present our two-step methodology: a quantitative, automated topic modelling analysis of journal article abstracts in which the phrases "mainstream media" or "mainstream journalis Ã " appear, and a qualitative context analysis of uses of "mainstream" in articles published in the main journalism journals from 2011 to 2022. After discussing our findings, we conclude that the rules of the mainstream language-game are confusing and need revisions, and that the concept only functions as an illusory boundary object, which we find problematic, given its increased significance.

"Mainstream" as a Language Game Used as a metaphor, "mainstream" has a myriad of potential meanings. It originates from a political context in the United States, where New York governor Nelson Rockefeller during the 1960s spoke about the "mainstream of political thought and action" (Safire 2008, 408). Rockefeller used the concept as a positive standard, something of high quality that other things could be measured and tested against. The phrase "mainstream media" was, according to Safire (2008, 209), coined by former US presidential candidate Ralph Nader in 1985. Nader first used it as a rather descriptive phrase, but later connected it with a critical perspective on media favouring the powerful elite and suppressing alternative, critical voices, and minority perspectives (Nader 2015). This understanding is similar to the one Chomsky discussed when he in 1997 asked "what makes mainstream media mainstream?." Chomsky's answer was enmeshed with critical perspectives on elitism, power preservation, and market logic, and that mainstream media will only "reflect the interests of the buyers and sellers, the institutions, and the power systems that are around them" (Chomsky 1997).

In recent years, the metaphor has been connected with aggressive far-right populist rhetoric associated with former US president Donald Trump, who notoriously talked about "mainstream media" with unmasked antipathy, often in connection with phrases like "fake news" and "lamestream media." This aggressive, political rhetoric and the relations set up by it between what is mainstream and what is not, has to varying degrees been exported to other cultural contexts beyond the USA. But "mainstream media" no doubt refers to different media in different contexts, and the relations to political rhetoric will vary across and within different media systems.

"Mainstream" can be used as a descriptive concept, simply referring to the biggest and most influential news media in a media system, and as a normative concept, reflecting power dynamics and media criticism coming from all sides of the political spectrum. And both these uses will carry different meanings in different cultural and political contexts. To unpack these meanings, one must therefore look at how and in what contexts the concept is used. In language studies, analysing the meaning of words through their use is the starting point of speech act theory, in which a key premise is that one cannot derive the meaning of words inductively from some theory of meaning-making alone. Words and language are not "bloodless abstract objects that can be studied like specimens under a microscope" (Lycan 2018, 76). Words get their meaning in connection with behaviour, with the social practice of communication. Our investigation of uses of "mainstream" is guided by a particular view on how meaning-making happens in language. This view is based on Wittgenstein's philosophy of language and his notion of "language-games" (Wittgenstein [1953] 2009).

Wittgenstein's idea of meaning-making as a language-game implies that what we do when we use language in social acts of communication, is to make moves, and that these moves are governed by rules, just like the rules of other games. Wittgenstein's use of "game" should be interpreted metaphorically. Black (1979, 350) argues that language-game "should be regarded as an Idiosyncratic term of art derived from ordinary uses of, say, 'game with words', by a deliberate extension of application." One important difference between language-games and other games, like football or chess, is that in language-games the rules are very complex and rarely articulated. We learn the rules of language-games as we learn to speak. We learn that certain acts of communication, certain moves, are appropriate responses to certain other communicative moves. The phrase "You're welcome" is, for instance, an appropriate response to the phrase "Thank you", and it gets its meaning as a response to the "Thank you." Such rules, which regulate what is considered appropriate responses to certain acts of communication are essential for the communication to make sense. Without relations to other communicative moves, a single speech-act rarely makes much sense. Meaning, thereby, is the function words and phrases perform in the context of social practice. This is also the case with a word like "mainstream." This word predominantly carries meaning through its relations with other speech acts in a given social context. Knowledge of both the context of use and the relations to other speech acts is therefore essential to understand what "mainstream" means. Viewing uses of "mainstream" as communicative moves in language-games implies an emphasis on the purpose of the move within the given context, and an evaluation of it (Deitz and Arrington 1984, 5). Like moves in a game, communicative actions are goal oriented. We must assume that scholars in media, communication, and journalism studies (and any other actor for that matter) use the term "mainstream" with intent. Its use serves a specific purpose, and revealing this purpose is one of the aims of analysing the mainstream language-game. Moreover, like moves in any other game, uses of mainstream can be evaluated as appropriate or in-appropriate. Appropriate moves follow the rules of the game, while in-appropriate moves violate the rules.

In our analysis of the mainstream language-game, as it is played in published journal articles, we take for granted that the uses of mainstream are already evaluated as appropriate through peer-review processes and editorial evaluation and that these uses thereby adhere to the rules of the mainstream language-game. Published journal articles not only follow the rules of academic publishing, they also follow the rules of the many language-games embedded in them, related to the topics they treat and the key words they apply. As such, published journal articles follow the rules of the field of academic publishing also in a Bourdieuian sense, implying that belonging to such a field means adhering to its rules and sharing "implicit schemes of classification" (Bourdieu 2005, 36). Uses of "mainstream" in the context of academic publishing could resemble such an implicit classification, which would mean that the relational aspects of its use are more implicit than explicit. Uses of "mainstream" can in such cases refer to an in-field common-sensicle meaning of the word which media, communication, and journalism scholars assume is recognized by their colleagues as part of the field's doxa. This does not mean that the concept is used in a non-relational way, it only means that relational dimensions are implicit. In our analysis, we will therefore look for such implicit communicative moves.

Given this premise that the uses of "mainstream" in published journal articles are already deemed appropriate, an analysis of how the mainstream language-game is played in this context will reveal the rules that govern this game. Such an analysis will potentially also reveal if the rules have changed, and if there are conflicting rules, which would suggest that there is not one mainstream language-game, but several. It follows from the aforementioned that analysing uses of mainstream as a language-game will require taking the following aspectswhich are also proposed by Deitz and Arrington's (1984, 5) operationalisation of language-game analysisinto account:

The interrelated actions of uses of "mainstream" and other speech acts, the context of use, the purpose of the use, and the appropriate mode of evaluation. We address these aspects through an analysis of the following research question: RQ1: What are the topical contexts and relations in which "mainstream" follows an "appropriate" language-game use in media, communication, and journalism studies research?

"Mainstream" as a Boundary Object Analysing uses of mainstream as a language-game only makes sense if this game is important in a broader context. There are several reasons why we believe the mainstream language-game is important. First, it points to some key characteristics of the news media: who and what is considered at the centre and who and what is not. Second, the word "mainstream" is often associated with words like "elite," "power," and "hegemony" (Chomsky 1997), or with words like "traditional" and "established" (Cushion, McDowell-Naylor, and Thomas 2021) and its use can therefore potentially reveal something about perceptions of such dimensions of the news media. Third, "mainstream" is also connected to political discourse, not only because the word's origin as a metaphor lies with such a discourse, but also because it has been further politicised in recent years by the alternative right movement (Steppat, Castro, and Esser 2021). Fourth, "mainstream" is a common word also in popular discourse and with actors in the media industry, which means it is used in multiple language-games also outside of academia and politics. "Mainstream" is therefore a word that seems to create a link between academic, professional, political, and popular discourse and the various actors involved in all those discourses. As such, it seems to constitute a shared space for communicative actionseven though the word might mean different things to the different actors and in differing contexts. This is exactly the characteristic of what Star labelled a "boundary object" (Star 1988;Star and Griesemer 1989). Boundary objects are "objects which are both plastic enough to adapt to local needs and the constraints of the several parties employing them, yet robust enough to maintain a common identity across sites." They have "different meanings in different social worlds but their structure is common enough to more than one world to make them recognizable, a means of translation" (Star and Griesemer 1989, 393). "Mainstream" might serve as such a flexible translator between social worlds. It seems to have a common-sensical meaning, which makes it identifiable across contexts, and it is filled with additional layers of meaning when used in local contexts. This flexibility ideally allows discourses in academia, politics, the media industry, and among the general public around for instance news media and power to overlap.

Boundary object was originally used as an analytical concept in studies of cooperative work in the absence of consensus, specifically how various professional groups managed to cooperate despite their different viewpoints (Star and Griesemer 1989).

Since its original use more than 30 years ago, "boundary object" has been applied to the analysis of many kinds of situations, practices, and socio-cultural phenomena in different academic disciplines and fields, including analysis of the meaning of words and phrases in academic scholarship. Examples include analysis of "Energy justice" as a boundary object in energy research (Iwi nska, Lis, and Ma˛czka 2021), "resilience" across academic disciplines (Baggio, Brown, and Hellebrandt 2015), "stewardship" in sustainability research (Pec ¸anha Enqvist et al. 2018), and "science communication" as a boundary object connecting science and nonscience (Horst 2022). Star (2010, 612-613) argues that the concept of boundary objects is most useful at the organisational level, where the work arrangements that form around them can be studied. This points to the origin of the concept within ethnographic fieldwork and that boundary objects are most relevant as something people act in relation to, in a defined social context. As such, treating "mainstream" as a potential boundary object connecting the social worlds of news production, academic work, politics, and popular discourse might be perceived as stretching the concept too far, scaled up to such a degree that its analytical power collapses, like a balloon filled with too much air. However, our analysis of mainstream as a possible boundary object is scaled down to the communicative actions within one, limited social world, the one of academic journal publishing in a specific field. The other social worlds to which "mainstream" plays a role are visible as discursive and empirical traces within this limited context of academic publishing. For example, media and journalism scholars might in their publications investigate how actors within or outside of mainstream news media perform or reflect, they might investigate political discourse within or outside of mainstream news media, they might investigate uses and perceptions of mainstream media among publics, and so on and so forth. Moreover, combining boundary object analysis with language-game analysis makes it possible to scale up the analytical ambition and study the "ambiguity of words'' across social worlds (Star 2010, 612).

Our analysis is not a typical boundary object study. We do not do ethnographic fieldwork and as such do not study the work arrangements of the various actors involved in academic publishing and how "mainstream" might work as a boundary object to them. We analyse how "mainstream" as a concept is negotiated and given meaning in academic publications. Meaning can take many forms in this context, ranging from the common-sensical and rather vague meaning that serves as a potential translator between social worlds, to the more specified and nuanced meanings that occur in the local contexts of empirical data. This will partly be addressed by RQ1, but will be further explored through a qualitative analysis of a subset of journal articles within journalism studies journals only suited to address a second research question:

RQ 2: How is the word "mainstream" defined and given nuanced meaning in journalism studies journals?

Viewed together, the two RQs provide findings that address this article's main aim, which is to analyse whether "mainstream" functions as a boundary object in academic discourse on media and journalism.

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
paragraphs: To answer the RQs, we utilised a mixture of computational and qualitative text analysis of journal articles emphasising "mainstream" as a key concept. The corpus of articles was identified through a search in the Scopus database, through which all original articles in media, communication and journalism journals which mentioned either of the phrases "mainstream media" or "mainstream journalis Ã " in the title, the abstract, or as a keyword was identified. We identified journals not through database categorisation, but by including all journals that have one or more of the words "media," "communication," or "journalis Ã " in their title. The search yielded 790 articles published in 134 different journals between 1990 and 2022. We chose this broad range of journals because digital journalism studies is an interdisciplinary field characterised by, among other things, the blurring of boundaries between journalism studies and other academic fields (Steensen and Westlund 2021). As such, digital journalism scholarship finds it home not only in journalism journals, but in a potentially wide range of journals within media and communication and beyond.

Only 1% of the 790 articles (11 articles) was published before 2000, and only 16% (126 articles) before 2011. This suggests that use of the concept "mainstream" in relation to media and journalism is a quite recent phenomenon. To check if the concept "mainstream media" has increased in significance compared to other concepts that might be perceived as synonyms, we performed additional searches where we replaced "mainstream" with "traditional," "legacy," "established," and "professional."

The results, which can be seen in Figure 1, clearly indicated that "mainstream" has risen to become the most used of these concepts in recent years in media, communication, and journalism journals. Articles in media, communication, and journalism journals that mention "mainstream media," "traditional media," "legacy media," "professional media," or "established media" in the title, abstract, or as a keyword. Searches were performed in the Scopus database 16 December 2022 with the following search string (in which "mainstream" was replaced with the other words for the additional searches): (TITLE-ABS-KEY ("Mainstream media") AND SRCTITLE (journalis Ã ) OR SRCTITLE (media) OR SRCTITLE (communication)) AND (LIMIT-TO (DOCTYPE, "ar") OR LIMIT-TO (DOCTYPE, "re").

The four journals with most articles in our "mainstream" corpus were Journalism -Theory, Practice & Criticism (42 articles), Journalism Practice (41 articles), Journalism Studies (36 articles), and Digital journalism (31 articles). This implies that journalism studies is the main field within media and communication studies for discussions of mainstream media and journalism. There are 14 journalism studies journals in the corpus (i.e., journals with the word "journalism" in their name), and these account for 30% of the total articles.

We performed two types of analysis on this corpus. First, and to find the topics these articles treated, and the relations set up in them (RQ1), we performed a topic modelling analysis of all abstracts. Second, and to find out how the word "mainstream" is given meaning in journalism studies in particular (RQ2), we performed a qualitative context analysis of paragraphs in which the word "mainstream" occurred in articles published in the five most significant journalism journals (in terms of number of articles in the corpus): Journalism -Theory, Practice & Criticism, Journalism Practice, Journalism Studies, Digital journalism, and Journalism and Mass Communication Quarterly. Both methods will be detailed below.

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
paragraphs: Topic modelling is an automated textual analysis method to identify dominant topics in large corpuses of texts. The most common topic modelling technique is Latens Dirchlet Alocation (LDA), which is a generative, unsupervised statistical model that automatically creates "topics," that is, clusters of words, from a collection of documents (Jacobi, van Atteveldt, and Welbers 2016). We used an application called the Topic Modelling Tool (Enderle [2015] 2022) to run a LDA model on the abstracts from the 779 articles in our corpus published from 2000 and beyond. It works as follows: Words in each document are randomly assigned to a specified number of topics. The algorithm then goes through several iterations and tries to refine the model of which terms are best suited to which topics, based on co-occurrence in the documents.

Topic modelling works best on shorter documents, which is why we chose abstracts instead of full articles. A common problem with unsupervised topic modelling such as LDA techniques is how to decide how many topics to identify, since this has to be determined before the model can be run. Sbalchiero and Eder (2020) suggests that 15-16 topics is the best fit for a corpus such as ours. We therefore first ran the model with 16 topics but discovered quite much overlap between these topics. This has probably to do with the fact that our corpus consists of texts that are thematically similar (they all deal with "mainstream" in relation to media and/or journalism). We therefore decided to lower the number of topics to ten, which is consistent with the number of topics used by Nylander, Fejes, & Milana (2022) in their topic model analysis of 1185 abstracts of journal articles from one specific journal. This produced much better results, with topics that were more distinct from one another.

The Topic Modelling Tool allows for exclusion of specific words before performing the topic modelling, and we used a general English stopword list to exclude so-called function words that don't carry as much meaning, such as determiners and prepositions (the, a, in, to, from, etc.). After a few test iterations, we found that expanding the stopword list with a few very dominant words throughout the corpus helped with creating meaningful topics. These excluded words were; article, paper, media, mainstream, study, analysis, journalism, journalist, journalists, news. Since all articles in our corpus deal with mainstream, media, and journalism, these words appeared in all topics generated by the modelling when included, thereby muting other important words. Excluding them helped create more distinct topics. We did, however, concatenate certain important word combinations (like "social media" and "new media") to preserve these combinations when running the model.

We set the model to run 400 iterations of ten topics with the expanded stopword list. The topics identified by the model were produced as a list of words that characterised each topic. These lists of words were inductively analysed to create meaningful topic labels.

Topic modelling is an automated textual analysis method to identify dominant topics in large corpuses of texts. The most common topic modelling technique is Latens Dirchlet Alocation (LDA), which is a generative, unsupervised statistical model that automatically creates "topics," that is, clusters of words, from a collection of documents (Jacobi, van Atteveldt, and Welbers 2016). We used an application called the Topic Modelling Tool (Enderle [2015] 2022) to run a LDA model on the abstracts from the 779 articles in our corpus published from 2000 and beyond. It works as follows: Words in each document are randomly assigned to a specified number of topics. The algorithm then goes through several iterations and tries to refine the model of which terms are best suited to which topics, based on co-occurrence in the documents.

Topic modelling works best on shorter documents, which is why we chose abstracts instead of full articles. A common problem with unsupervised topic modelling such as LDA techniques is how to decide how many topics to identify, since this has to be determined before the model can be run. Sbalchiero and Eder (2020) suggests that 15-16 topics is the best fit for a corpus such as ours. We therefore first ran the model with 16 topics but discovered quite much overlap between these topics. This has probably to do with the fact that our corpus consists of texts that are thematically similar (they all deal with "mainstream" in relation to media and/or journalism). We therefore decided to lower the number of topics to ten, which is consistent with the number of topics used by Nylander, Fejes, & Milana (2022) in their topic model analysis of 1185 abstracts of journal articles from one specific journal. This produced much better results, with topics that were more distinct from one another.

The Topic Modelling Tool allows for exclusion of specific words before performing the topic modelling, and we used a general English stopword list to exclude so-called function words that don't carry as much meaning, such as determiners and prepositions (the, a, in, to, from, etc.). After a few test iterations, we found that expanding the stopword list with a few very dominant words throughout the corpus helped with creating meaningful topics. These excluded words were; article, paper, media, mainstream, study, analysis, journalism, journalist, journalists, news. Since all articles in our corpus deal with mainstream, media, and journalism, these words appeared in all topics generated by the modelling when included, thereby muting other important words. Excluding them helped create more distinct topics. We did, however, concatenate certain important word combinations (like "social media" and "new media") to preserve these combinations when running the model.

We set the model to run 400 iterations of ten topics with the expanded stopword list. The topics identified by the model were produced as a list of words that characterised each topic. These lists of words were inductively analysed to create meaningful topic labels.

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
paragraphs: To get a deeper understanding of how the word "mainstream" is used and contextualised in journalism studies specifically, we performed a qualitative context analysis of the articles in the corpus which were published from 2011 and onwards in the five most dominant journalism journals. This sub-set consisted of 130 articles (37 articles in Journalism Practice, 32 articles in Journalism -Theory, Practice & Criticism, 32 articles in Digital journalism, 25 articles in Journalism Studies, and 4 articles in Journalism and Mass Communication Quarterly).

To help identify the contexts in which the word "mainstream" appeared in these articles, we used an online, open access text analysis application called Voyant tools (Sinclair and Rockwell 2016). In Voyant tools we used the tool called "context," to extract the words from a text corpus occurring before and after the specified search word term. To do this, we uploaded pdf versions of all the 130 articles to Voyant tools, after first removing the bibliographies, tables, figures, and metadata. We set the tool to extract 50 words before and 50 words after each occurrence of "mainstream." The results were downloaded as a tab separated file and imported to Excel for further analysis. Some data cleaning was necessary to perform in Excel, mostly related to character encoding. The data consisted of 3008 occurrences of the word "mainstream." We first read the contexts before and after all occurrences of "mainstream" in each article to analyse how and to what extent it was defined, looking particularly at whether "mainstream" appeared in relation to words like "defined as," "meaning," "referring to," "understood as," or "operationalised." If no clear definition was offered, we examined if other, less clearly formulated, or indirect/implicit meanings were present. Furthermore, we investigated the normative context of "mainstream" by looking for evaluative formulations made by either the author, the literature or theories used, or by the object of study.

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
paragraphs: The topic modelling analysis provides us with an overview of the topical contexts in which "mainstream" is used in media and communication journals, while also indicating the relational dimensions that are considered appropriate uses of the concept. We therefore start with the findings of our topic modelling analysis, before we dive deeper into the meaning-making processes established by the uses of mainstream in journalism journals specifically.

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
sections.header_raw: Topics and Relations in the Mainstream Language Game
sections.header_norm: topics and relations in the mainstream language game
paragraphs: Table 1 presents the ten topics identified through the topic modelling LDA technique. The "topic coverage" column in Table 1 indicates how much of the whole text corpus belongs to each topic. This should not be interpreted as how many abstracts belong to each topic, since most abstracts have content that belongs to more than one topic. The "Increase/decrease over time" column indicates if the topic increases or decreases in significance during the years from 2000 to 2022. This is measured as correlation (scale from À1 to 1) between years and topic coverage per year. Since all these numbers are quite close to zero, there is really no big difference in significance of topics throughout the period. The only significant differences are the increases for topic 3: Politics, audiences and sources and topic 6: Social media/social movement, and the decrease for topic 4: Alternative journalism for the whole corpus, but the differences over time for all these topics are very small.

There are five topics that each account for more than ten percent of the whole text corpus, implying that these five topics are more significant than the others. This applies to the whole corpus as well as the sub-set published in the five top journalism journals.

The topics identified clearly indicate that uses of "mainstream" in media, communication, and journalism journals are closely linked to the social worlds of politics and publics, and to a certain degree the combination of those in for example social movements. Relations are typically established between mainstream media on the one side and social, alternative or community media on the other. "Mainstream" seems also to be connected with professionalism (and thereby as an assumed opposite of amateurs), and with specific issues of coverage, like for instance climate change, which is a main issue in topic 2, gender and health (topic 8), war and conflict (topic 9), and minority representations (topic 7). These relations thereby constitute appropriate uses of mainstream within the context of academic publishing in media, communication, and journalism journals, and as such reveal, at least in part, the rules that govern the mainstream language-game within this context.

However, we must dive deeper into these relations to understand the processes of meaning-making in this language-game. Next, we therefore turn to the qualitative context analysis of uses of "mainstream" in a subset of the articles published in journalism journals. First, we will look at degrees of explicit meaning-making and coherence, i.e. the degrees to which "mainstream" is defined. Then we turn to a deeper analysis of the relational positions the concept is given. 1

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
sections.header_raw: Degrees of Coherence in the Mainstream Language Game
sections.header_norm: degrees of coherence in the mainstream language game
paragraphs: In the 3008 instances of "mainstream" in the 130 journalism journal articles in our corpus published from 2011 and onwards, we identify four tendencies related to degrees "Topic labels" are the authors' interpretation of the commonality between the topic words, which are listed in column 2 in order of significance to the topic. "Topic coverage" is the percentage of the whole text corpus that belongs to each topic, while "Increase/decrease over time" is correlation between years and topic coverage per year. Significant results are marked with Ã p < 0.05 or ÃÃ p < 0.01.

of definition and thereby coherence. The first and most predominant is that mainstream media is seldom defined or operationalized throughout the period. In this sense it can be said to have a common-sensical meaning amongst journalism scholars, who seem to presume that other scholars and readers share the same understanding. Such use suggests that the word serves as a translator between various social worlds.

The obvious benefit of such use is that the research seemingly becomes accessible across worlds, while the downside is a lack of clarity and nuance within the academic discourse specifically since the meaning of "mainstream media" may differ greatly in specific media systems. These understandings may be influenced by the readers' previous knowledge about the country or region in which the study is situatedbut there is still a danger that nuances are lost. Second, authors often combine "mainstream" with other concepts connected to (i) genre and content, e.g. news, journalism, photojournalism, coverage (Awad 2011;Baroni 2015;Wu 2021); (ii) practices, e.g. reporting, performance, culture (Klawier, Prochazka, and Schweiger 2022;Morton 2015;Nygren et al. 2018); (iii) groups, e.g., journalists, news workers, photojournalists, commentators, audiences (Baroni 2015;Nygaard 2021); (iv) structural dimensions, e.g., commercial, organisations, institutions, sites (Harlow 2022;Kenix 2013); or (v) nationality, e.g., Nigerian, Israeli, American (Akinfemisoye 2014;Lachover 2021;Miller 2012). The combinations of words point to the flexibility and malleability of "mainstream" as a potential boundary object, and how it can be filled with additional layers of meaning when used in local contexts.

Third, "mainstream" is often understood relationally and implicitly. This is particularly the case when the word is referred to in the context of similar or competing phenomena, most predominantly social media (Lasorsa, Lewis, and Holton 2012), citizen journalism (Carr et al. 2014), indigenous media (Hanusch 2014), hyper-local news (Hess and Waller 2016), or community and alternative media (Andersen, Shehata, and Andersson 2021;Cushion 2022;Harcup 2016;Malik 2015). In these instances, "mainstream" is often contextualised around blurry boundaries and hybridity, pointing to how matters of definition are not clear-cut. The meaning of "mainstream media" is here defined by what it is not rather than what it is. This can be expressed by negation (i.e., "non-mainstream") or indirectly by describing another phenomenon. To illustrate: when alternative or community media are characterised as non-hierarchical, participatory, and representing ordinary people, it indicates that the mainstream media is hierarchical, exclusionary, and elitist. These binary (and at the same time blurry) relations create meaning through the researchers' nuanced perceptions of the mainstream media as an implicit benchmark for how they understand other media.

Finally, in the few instances where scholars more explicitly describe or operationalise "mainstream media" or "mainstream journalism," macro, meso, and micro dimensions are emphasised and outlined in more detail, including the purpose and function, e.g., the democratic and societal role of mainstream media (Baroni 2015;Berkowitz and Schwartz 2016;Holt, Figenschou, and Frischlich 2019;Van Dalen 2021;Miller 2012); questions of professionalism in journalism related for instance to facts, ethics, or objectivity (Aharoni and Lissitsa 2022;Moyo 2011); the structural dimensions of mainstream media, e.g. hierarchical, commercial, public, state, private (Holt, Figenschou, and Frischlich 2019;Reiter and Matthes 2021;Guo and Zhang 2020); or by referring to concrete actors like for instance The New York Times, The BBC, The Guardian (Lawrence and Schafer 2012;Reiter and Matthes 2021;S ¸ahin 2018). These concrete specifications make the concept identifiable across social worlds, but also show how different dimensions are given weight in journalism studies.

Together, these forms of meaning-making indicate that different theoretical, analytical, and geo-political contexts are important to how "mainstream" is given meaning, and that these differences affect understandings of the word within journalism studies. Although some shared understanding is found, "mainstream" lacks a definitional core. As such, "mainstream" starts to crumble as a boundary object, because there might not exist a common-sensicle meaning of the word that cuts across social worlds. This, in turn, indicates that there is not one mainstream language-game in media and communication scholarship, not even in journalism studies. There might be several such games, depending on where the use of the concept draws its empirical references.

These binary (and at the same time blurry) relations create meaning through the researchers' nuanced perceptions of the mainstream media as an implicit benchmark for how they understand other media.

Finally, in the few instances where scholars more explicitly describe or operationalise "mainstream media" or "mainstream journalism," macro, meso, and micro dimensions are emphasised and outlined in more detail, including the purpose and function, e.g., the democratic and societal role of mainstream media (Baroni 2015;Berkowitz and Schwartz 2016;Holt, Figenschou, and Frischlich 2019;Van Dalen 2021;Miller 2012); questions of professionalism in journalism related for instance to facts, ethics, or objectivity (Aharoni and Lissitsa 2022;Moyo 2011); the structural dimensions of mainstream media, e.g. hierarchical, commercial, public, state, private (Holt, Figenschou, and Frischlich 2019;Reiter and Matthes 2021;Guo and Zhang 2020); or by referring to concrete actors like for instance The New York Times, The BBC, The Guardian (Lawrence and Schafer 2012;Reiter and Matthes 2021;S ¸ahin 2018). These concrete specifications make the concept identifiable across social worlds, but also show how different dimensions are given weight in journalism studies.

Together, these forms of meaning-making indicate that different theoretical, analytical, and geo-political contexts are important to how "mainstream" is given meaning, and that these differences affect understandings of the word within journalism studies. Although some shared understanding is found, "mainstream" lacks a definitional core. As such, "mainstream" starts to crumble as a boundary object, because there might not exist a common-sensicle meaning of the word that cuts across social worlds. This, in turn, indicates that there is not one mainstream language-game in media and communication scholarship, not even in journalism studies. There might be several such games, depending on where the use of the concept draws its empirical references.

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
sections.header_raw: Position and Purpose in the Mainstream Language Game
sections.header_norm: position and purpose in the mainstream language game
paragraphs: To further unpack the complex ambiguity of uses of the term "mainstream" in journalism studies, we will now take a closer look at the normative positions and purposes of these uses in journalism journals from 2011 to today. The normative positions can be expressed by the authors of the articles, in the analytical frameworks of the articles or by the objects of analysis. Overall, we find three main approaches: Mainstream media's (inadequate) use of emerging digital technologies: One approach that is found throughout the period are studies of how and to what extent new digital technologies change journalism. These studies often examine to what extent mainstream media adopts technological innovations or compare mainstream media and social media agendas, sources, and frames. Here, there is an underlying normative expectation that mainstream media should include and integrate networked technology to increase popular participation and democratise mainstream journalism (e.g. Vis 2013; Garden 2016). The perceived positive impact of adopting new networked technology becomes particularly important in media systems where the mainstream media are part of repressive structures. Several of the technology-oriented studies are situated in authoritarian or semi-authoritarian political contexts (e.g., Iran, Egypt, Turkey, China, Singapore, Hong Kong, Latin America, Zimbabwe). Here the mainstream media represent stagnant, inaccessible, protocol news tightly controlled by the political (and economic) ruling elites. Furthermore, researchers analyse how new digital media both challenge existing structures and seek to merge mainstream and newer bottom-up practices into new hybrid forms (e.g. Wu 2021;Allam 2019). In these contexts, poor standards, low trust, and lack of voice and dialogue with state-controlled mainstream media are used to explain and motivate the emergence of digital, hybrid forms of journalism. The "mainstream" is used as a negative benchmark, employed both by authors to describe key features of the media system studied and by actors studied.

Mainstream media as representing hegemonic interests: Another group of studies approach the "mainstream" through critical theoretical frameworks such as (critical) political economy, hegemony, or mediated marginalisation. This criticism of the mainstream media is linked to the media's elitism, corporate ownership (neoliberalism, commercialism) and/or tight political-media connections and are found throughout the period studied. The studies written from a hegemony approach criticise mainstream media for being neoliberalists (Basu 2019;Petley 2021) or representing national military or security interests (Kennis 2019). Other studies analyse how the mainstream media is suppressing dissidents and social movements (e.g. Brown, Mourao, and Sylvie 2019;Harlow et al. 2020). Related to these discussions of hegemonic, elitist media, another dominant criticism is that mainstream media marginalises different ethnic or religious minority communities (Awad 2011;S ¸ahin 2018;Yu 2017). These normative positions are based on critical analysis of the mainstream media as the primary object of analysis. The critique is most unilateral in studies using textual and discourse analysis, whereas interview-based studies with reporters and audiences highlight dilemmas, contradictions, and nuances more often (e.g., Hanusch 2014; Lachover 2021). Overall, these articles conduct critical discussions on the mainstream media's failure to meet its normative, societal democratic functions (particularly the watchdog role and its responsibility to facilitate democratic debate).

Mainstream media as negative benchmark: A third group of studies do not have the "mainstream" as the primary object of analysis but use it as a (primarily negative) benchmark to understand new forms of journalism (e.g., citizen journalism, community media, alternative media). Over the period analysed, the mainstream is contrasted to different types of journalistic initiatives. We find a gradual ideological turn from studies relating the mainstream to progressive left-wing initiatives to radical right initiatives. From the 2011 to 2016 studies, citizen or community media are foregrounded as participatory, non-hierarchical, self-managed, democratic, and representative of ordinary people (e.g., Baroni 2015;Malik 2015). This implicitly indicates the shortcomings and the inadequacies of the mainstream media, but also discusses these forms of journalism as complementary. From 2017 and onwards, conservative to far-right media initiatives gain prominence as objects of analysis (e.g. Holt et al, 2019). The far-right challengers take a clear antagonistic, oppositional position, criticising the mainstream media for being politically biased (left-leaning, politically correct, liberal), distanced from real people's problems (elitist, corrupt), ideologically non-inclusive and lacking plurality (discriminating, censor critics, double-standard) (e.g. Mayerh€ offer 2021; el-Nawawy and Hamas Elmasry 2021; Van Dalen 2021). While these studies demonstrate strong criticism against the mainstream media and political establishment from the objects of analysis, the authors' themselves are critical towards the actors they study and concerned about the negative longer-term effects of demonising the mainstream (e.g., mistrust, polarisation, disinformation) (e.g. Andersen, Shehata, and Andersson 2021; Reiter and Matthes 2021)

sections.paper_id: Steensen et al. - 2023 - Playing the Mainstream Game. A Language-Game Analysis of “Mainstream” as a Possible Boundary Object
paragraphs: In this article, we argue that it is essential to critically examine how we as journalism scholars are part of ongoing language games in which we give concepts meanings through social acts of communications. Such meaning-making processes are on one level impacted by the wider political and social contexts, but on another level, scholars are also key players in how certain understandings are constructed. Our analysis demonstrates that "mainstream" is a frequently used concept which has multiple, yet interrelated meanings and serves a variety of purposes in media and communication studies in general and (digital) journalism studies in particular.

Our analysis of RQ1 -What are the topical contexts and relations in which "mainstream" follows an "appropriate" language-game use in media, communication, and journalism studies research?suggest that the rules of the mainstream media language-game in academic scholarship seem fairly comprehensible. In our topic modelling analysis of articles in which "mainstream media" or "mainstream journalis Ã " are key concepts we find that the dominant relations are those between the mainstream on the one hand and social, alternative and community media on the other. Moreover, the word is used in relation to politics, audiences, and special issues of coverage, like climate change, minority representation, health, and war. These relations seem fairly well connected with a common-sensical understanding of mainstream media/journalism. The lack of definitions we found in our context analysis of uses of mainstream in journalism journals, suggest that it is such a common-sensical understanding of the concept that dominates this academic discourse. The meaning of the word is often presupposed and thereby taken for granted, which suggests that journalism scholars seem to feel confident that such a common-sensical understanding of the concept does exist.

However, our analysis reveals that this can be a problematic assumption. When investigating RQ2 -How is the word "mainstream" defined and given nuanced meaning in journalism journals?we find that the meanings of "mainstream" are highly dependent on how the word is combined with other concepts, as well as contextual factors related to theoretical and geo-political dimensions. The concept is predominantly used to implicitly or explicitly criticise how the major, dominant media performs its societal democratic functions, yet what shortcomings are addressed and how they are explained depend on the cultural and political context, the theoretical framework employed, and the actors studied. These dependencies become especially evident in our analysis of the normative positions embedded in uses of "mainstream" in journalism studies over the last decade. With these positions, scholars approach "mainstream media" in the context of emerging digital technologies, hegemonic perspectives, and alternative forms of journalism. What these normative positions have in common is a critical attitude towards the "mainstream," however, importantly these criticisms can be raised through the voice of the author(s), the theoretical frameworks used in the articles and through the objects of analysis.

Our analysis clearly demonstrates that "mainstream" lacks a definitional core. We therefore conclude that "mainstream" is used as a concept in not one, but several language-games played by media, communication, and journalism scholarseven though it might be perceived as the same game by both authors and readers. This means that while "mainstream" may be used as a boundary object, it may be given the significance of a boundary object, and it may be perceived by various groups in various social worlds as a boundary objectit is not a boundary object. Instead, "mainstream" functions as an illusory boundary object, a mirage that easily can create confusion. When digging into the layers of meaning the word achieves in use it becomes clear that even though it has multiple meanings, which each make sense in local contexts, it does not have one, shared, common-sensicle meaning that cuts across the boundaries between various journalistic contexts, let alone the various social worlds in politics, in the media ecosystems, in academia, and among audiences. The word is at best ambiguous, at worst a metaphorical misconception.

We end this article by suggesting that the rules of the "mainstream languagegame," as it is played in academic discourse, need some adjustments and increased awareness in the future. Authors, reviewers, and journal editors should not take the meaning of the concepts "mainstream media" or "mainstream journalism" for granted. As there is not a shared, common-sensicle understanding of these concepts, the scholarly discourse must strive to make transparent the contexts, the relations, the purposes, and the normative underpinnings set up by uses of "mainstream." This is important for two reasons: First, the word's increased significance not only in journalism studies, but in wider academic circles and in broader political and popular discourse. Second, because "mainstream" in recent years has become increasingly politicised and weaponised by influential antagonistic challengers of journalism.

Finally, we argue that this call for conceptual clarity goes beyond the concept of "mainstream." In its relatively young history, digital journalism studies has been preoccupied with questioning established truths about what journalism is, who produces it and what roles it plays by giving much attention to fringe actors and practices (Steensen and Westlund 2021). Perhaps it is now time to return to a core, to a new mainstream.

In its relatively young history, digital journalism studies has been preoccupied with questioning established truths about what journalism is, who produces it and what roles it plays by giving much attention to fringe actors and practices (Steensen and Westlund 2021). Perhaps it is now time to return to a core, to a new mainstream.
